title: Class Weights: Penalizing Mistakes
concept: Telling the algorithm's cost function to penalize minority mistakes much more than majority mistakes; unequal penalty analogy.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which method balances a dataset by generating synthetic, intermediate data points?
exercises.explain: Correct! SMOTE draws lines between existing points to generate new synthetic examples.

cards.title: The high-stakes test
cards.body: Imagine taking an exam where normal questions are worth 1 point, but one rare question is worth 100 points. Where would you focus your study time? You'd make absolutely sure you got that 100-point question right!

cards.title: What are Class Weights?
cards.body: Class weights are settings in a machine learning algorithm that tell the model how much to care about mistakes. By assigning a higher "weight" (penalty) to the minority class, we make mistakes on rare items much more costly.

cards.title: The unequal penalty
cards.body: Imagine a goalie in soccer. Letting in a goal in a friendly practice match is a minor mistake. Letting in a goal in the World Cup final is a massive disaster. The goalie pays way more attention in the final!

cards.title: Medical Diagnoses
cards.body: If a doctor misses a common cold, the patient is slightly annoyed. If a doctor misses a rare, life-threatening illness, it is a catastrophe. In AI, we assign a 1,000x penalty for missing the rare illness.

cards.title: We must always change the data to balance it
cards.body: You don't have to add or delete rows to handle imbalance. Class weights balance the attention of the model during learning, leaving the original dataset completely untouched.

cards.title: Equal vs. Weighted Penalty
cards.body: How penalty sizes adjust model attention:

left.text:
Every mistake costs 1 point.
Model ignores the rare class
because it's too small to affect total score.

right.text:
Minority mistake costs 100 points.
Model focuses heavily on rare class
to avoid huge penalty scores.

exercises.prompt: How do class weights help solve the imbalanced data problem?

options.text: By punishing the model more severely when it makes a mistake on the rare class.
By deleting rows from the majority class during training.
By scaling all numeric features between 0 and 1.

exercises.explain: Class weights increase the penalty for misclassifying the minority class, forcing the model to prioritize it.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Class weights modify the dataset by adding new rows.
exercises.explain: False. Class weights do not alter the data at all; they only change how the model's cost function evaluates mistakes.

exercises.sentence: Class weights change the penalty for mistakes without modifying the ___ itself.
exercises.explain: Class weights leave the original dataset untouched and adjust the training math.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario describes the use of class weights?

options.text: A model is told that misclassifying a rare disease scan is 50 times worse than misclassifying a healthy scan.
A programmer duplicates the rare disease scans 50 times.
A programmer deletes 50 healthy scans from the dataset.

exercises.explain: Setting a 50x penalty for rare mistakes is a class weighting strategy.

exercises.prompt: You are building an email spam filter. Only 1% of incoming emails are spam. You don't want to change your raw dataset, but you want to make sure the model focuses on finding spam. Which setting should you adjust?

options.text: Increase the weight (penalty) for misclassifying spam emails.
Scale the text features using Z-score standardization.
Convert the spam labels into continuous decimal values.
Decrease the weight for spam so the model ignores it.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Increasing the weight for the spam class makes spam mistakes very expensive for the model, forcing it to learn patterns that identify spam accurately. Next, how do we measure if our balanced model is actually doing a good job?
